ABSTRACT
Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) platforms are increasingly used to obtain plant phenotypes in crop breeding for 
their efficiency and versatility. A lightweight UAV was used to collect high-precision RGB images, multispectral 
and point cloud data of soybeans (Glycine max (L.) Merr.) across fields at various growth stages, utilizing an 
innovative cross-circling oblique (CCO) route. A multi-modal data fusion deep learning model was proposed 
based on the self-supervised contrastive learning strategy with fine-tuning for yield estimation and lodging 
discrimination in soybean germplasm resources. During the soybean growth stages of flowering (R1) to maturity 
(R8), the contrastive learning effectively captured the decoupling characteristics of different soybean varieties in 
the feature space. Higher accuracy in yield estimation was obtained combined contrastive learning with the 
traditional features. Correlations were significantly reduced between features among varieties (Pearson ’ s mean 
0.27 – 0.62) and feature separations were achieved after dimension reduction (R8: CH = 12.4, DB = 51.8). RMSE 
of yield estimation was 591.39 kg ha
� 1 
at high density and 532.75 kg ha
� 1 
at low density at R8 growth stages. 
Lodging discrimination achieved the highest accuracy with an F1-score of 0.57 at high density and 0.64 at low 
density. The results demonstrated that utilizing contrastive learning for extraction of deep soybean features holds 
significant potential in supporting traditional features for yield estimation and lodging discrimination.
1. Introduction
Soybean (Glycine max (L.) Merr.) is one of the most extensively 
cultivated oil crops worldwide. It plays an integral role in various food, 
feed, and industrial products ( Eugenio et al., 2020; Vogel et al., 2021 ). 
According to the statistics, soybean accounts for over 60 % of global 
vegetable oil and protein consumption and ranks as the fourth most 
productive field crop ( U.S. Department of Agriculture, 2019 ). Meeting 
the demands of a growing population and industrial production neces -
sitates enhancing soybean yield through breeding programs ( Liu et al., 
2020; Ren et al., 2023 ). Increasing planting density is an important way 
to increase yield per unit area. However, some of the soybean varieties 
tend to show yield declines under high planting density. Therefore, ac -
curate evaluation of phenotypic traits in soybean breeding lines is 
pivotal for efficiently screening of exceptional high-yield varieties ( Yu 
et al., 2016; Elsayed et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2020 ).
Crop breeding process necessitates the examination for more than 
hundreds of material varieties. Traditional field surveys are time- 
consuming, labor-intensive, and expensive with limited traits investi -
gation. In recent years, the rapid advancement of high-throughput 
phenotyping platforms and information technology-assisted crop 
phenotype detection has facilitated breeders in making informed de -
cisions ( Chivasa et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2023 ). 
Compared to satellite remote sensing, the unmanned aerial vehicle 
(UAV) proximity sensing platform exhibits characteristics of high effi -
ciency, low cost, and high spatial and temporal resolution. Therefore, 
extensive applications have been done in crop disease identification 
( Kerkech et al., 2018; Bhandari et al., 2020 ), lodging monitoring ( Song 
et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2020; Shu et al., 2023 ), chlorophyll content 
assessment ( Zhang et al., 2021; Qiao et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2022 ), as

2
well as biomass estimation ( Liu et al., 2019; Fei et al., 2021; Shu et al., 
2022; Togeirode Alckmin et al., 2022 ). To enhance the accuracy of yield 
estimation in soybean germplasm resources, Yoosefzadeh-Najafabadi et 
al (2021) acquired hyperspectral reflectance data from 250 soybean 
varieties at two growth stages and established vegetation indices, then 
utilized three machine learning methods to estimate yield. Similarly, Bai 
et al (2022) estimated the yield of over 1500 soybean varieties using a 
UAV equipped with RGB cameras. However, these approaches only 
focus on yield estimation and do not consider the phenotypic similarity 
among soybean germplasm resources with wide variation.
The rapid advancement of information technology has enabled the 
successful integration of deep learning with Unmanned Aerial Vehicle 
(UAV) data for the precise investigation of crop phenotypes ( Feng et al., 
2020; Vong et al., 2021; Ye et al., 2023 ). This combination has signifi -
cantly enhanced our ability to analyze soybean diseases ( Tetila et al., 
2020; Zhou et al., 2020 ) and estimate soybean yields ( Zhou et al., 2017; 
Maimaitijiang et al., 2020 ) through the use of UAV multimodal data and 
advanced deep learning techniques. Feature extraction, especially the 
extraction of deep features, plays a crucial role in this process. Deep 
features, derived from the intricate layers of neural networks, capture 
the underlying patterns and structures within the data, enabling more 
accurate predictions of crop phenotypes. These features are particularly 
valuable in estimating yield and detecting lodging levels under complex 
conditions, where traditional methods may fall short due to the high 
variability in crop varieties and environmental factors ( Mekonnen et al., 
2020; Vogel et al., 2021; Veas et al., 2022 ). However, conventional 
supervised learning approaches often require large amounts of anno -
tated training data, which can be labor-intensive and time-consuming to 
collect ( Chen et al., 2020; Jaiswal et al., 2021 ). This limitation restricts 
the generalizability of the learned features to specific tasks and datasets, 
making it difficult to transfer knowledge across different phenotypic 
traits ( Güldenring and Nalpantidis, 2021; Cao et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 
2023a ). To address these challenges, self-supervised learning methods 
have progressed rapidly ( He et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020; Grill et al., 
2020 ). These methods leverage contrastive learning techniques to 
generate latent feature representations from unlabeled data, facilitating 
the universal prediction of diverse phenotypic traits. By learning from 
the intrinsic structure of the data, self-supervised models can identify 
deep features that are robust to variations in crop varieties and envi -
ronmental conditions.
The diversity of crop varieties and environmental conditions in -
troduces significant variations in crop phenotypes ( Mekonnen et al., 
2020; Vogel et al., 2021; Veas et al., 2022 ). Factors such as the selection 
of crop varieties, microclimate conditions, and spatial variability of soil 
properties ( Kravchenko and Bullock, 2000; Peralta et al., 2016; Chivasa 
et al., 2021 ) further complicate the task of phenotype estimation. Fei 
et al. (2021) used ensemble learning algorithm based on multispectral 
images to estimate wheat yield under two irrigation conditions using 
vegetation index(VI) with heritability greater than 0.5. Ren et al. (2023)
utilized UAV-captured hyperspectral and RGB images to extract fea -
tures, including vegetation indices and texture, for various crop vari -
eties. Their analysis revealed that the heritability of vegetation indices 
varied across different growth stages, with hyperspectral indices 
showing higher heritability during the grain-filling stage. These findings 
highlight the need for advanced feature extraction methods that can 
effectively capture the complex relationships between crop phenotypes 
and environmental factors. Integrating contrastive learning into the 
feature extraction process allows us to fully exploit the rich information 
contained in multi-variety UAV multimodal data, leading to more ac -
curate and robust predictions of crop phenotypes.
The objectives of this study were to: (1) employ a contrastive 
learning strategy to construct a multi-stages and multi-modal data fusion 
learning task for UAV data, aiming to fully exploit the deep features and 
information embedded in germplasm data; (2) utilize decoupled features 
within the images to establish auxiliary tasks, enabling the exploration 
of feature expressions among different soybean varieties in diverse 
environments. (3) compare the performance of different approaches in 
terms of prediction accuracy, spatial robustness, and adaptability to 
various downstream tasks.
2. Material
2.1. Experimental location and setup
The experiment is located in Keshan County, Qiqihar City, Hei -
longjiang Province, China (48
◦
0 ′ 58.621 ″ N, 125
◦
49 ′ 47.213 ″ E). Keshan 
County of Qiqihar City ( Fig. 1 a) is located in the hinterland of Songnen 
Plain, one of the three major black soil belts in the world. It belongs to 
the cold temperate continental monsoon climate. In the study area 
( Fig. 1 c), a total of 263 soybean cultivars were planted with row distance 
of 0.35 m. The plant distance of 0.06 m for a low-density (D6) and 0.03 
m for a high-density (D3). Each plot covered an area of about 4.5 m
2 
for 
each cultivar ( Fig. 1 d). A total of 1,052 soybean plots were sown on May 
15, 2022 and harvested on October 10, 2022. Field management were 
conducted according to the local practices.
2.2. Data acquisition
2.2.1. Field data collection
Canopy height (CH), bottom pod height, main stem nodes branches, 
pods per plant, grains per plant, 100 seed weight and soybean yield were 
recorded in a total of 1052 plots. Lodging grade was assessed visually 
with five grades: no lodging (level 0), mild lodging (level 1), moderate 
lodging (level 3), severe lodging (level 5) and complete lodging (level 7). 
The soybean plots were individually harvested, threshed, and dried 
before being weighed at a moisture content below 13 %. The resulting 
yield was then expressed in kg ha
� 1
, as presented in Table 1 .
2.2.2. Image acquisition by multi-sensor mounted on UAV
The aerial survey was conducted using an UAV between the hours of 
11:00 am to 14:00 pm under clear and windless weather conditions on 
July 1st, July 18th, August 20th, and September 7th, 2022. Soybeans 
were at the flower bud stage (R1), pod stage (R3), filling stage (R6), and 
maturity stage (R8) respectively. The UAV utilizes the DJI Phantom 
4RTK equipped with the Parrot Sequoia multispectral camera (Parrot
